data.title: Athabasca Oil Corporation: Strong Cash Flow, Low Valuation, High Upside
data.description:
Despite the fact that the oil price has fallen to about $60 per barrel, Athabasca Oil Corporation (OTCPK:ATHOF)(TSX:ATH:CA) remains one of the most attractive value investments in the Canadian energy sector. With an impeccable balance sheet, low capital needs, 90+ years of reserves, and an aggressive capital repayment program, the company offers an asymmetrical risk and return profile.

Operations

Athabasca produces about 37,000 BOE/D, 95% of which consists of high marginal bitumen (heavy oil) from its long-term oil sand deposits. With main projects like Leismer and Hangingstone, the company can maintain high activity margins even at $60 per barrel.

The management stated that the company's break-even is about $40 WTI. At $60, the company still generates free cash flow, fully funding investment and the repurchase of shares. In contrast to shale companies, oil sand deposits have a very slow decline in extraction, so little investment is required to maintain production levels.

In 2024, capital spending was around CAD268 million, mainly dedicated to the development of Leismer and drilling in the Duvernay deposit. Even when investing in growth, the company generated CAD322 million free cash flow, which means more than 12% FCF yield.

Shareholder Value

From 2021, Athabasca returned more than $400 million of debt and now has a cash excess of about $145 million. Interest costs are low, and the remaining USD157 million debt can be fully covered without refinancing (the debt must be paid by 2026).

The company does not consider this money to be part of the reserve. In 2024 alone, it repurchased about $317 million in shares - more than 10% of their shares in circulation. The management promised to return 100% of the free cash flow to the shareholders in 2025 as well. The number of shares decreased from ~584 million in 2023 to ~518 million in 2024.

Valuation: Cheap

  • EV/EBITDA (2024): ~4.5x

  • P/E (2024): ~5.9x

  • FCF yield (2024): ~12%

  • P/B: ~1.3x, with 25%+ ROE

Most importantly, the net present value (NPV10) in the 2P reserves report is CAD6.4 billion - This is CAD12.44 per share when the share price is only around CAD 5. Even with risk and taxes, the market values ​​these shares at a huge discount.

The value of developed reserves (PDP) alone exceeds the entire company's enterprise value. This is the margin of safety that most E&P companies do not have.

Macroeconomics And Athabasca

At first glance, $60 per barrel seems bad. However, heavy oil producers in 2024 and 2025 had quite good news.

  • The differences between WTI-WCS narrowed to historic lows, sometimes reaching <10 USD
  • OPEC+ restrictions and sanctions for Russia/Venezuela restricted heavy oil supply.
  • Launching TMX Pipeline in 2025 added 590 thousand BBL/D export power, which is a structural improvement in prices.

Athabasca has resulted in the improvement of bituminous oil realization, even at lower WTI prices. In Q4 2024, its netback was CA 46 per barrel compared to just ~CAD30 a year earlier.

This is an invisible factor for gaining profit, which is often forgotten by investors. Even at $60, Athabasca generates strong profits.

Athabasca has 90+ years of 2P reserves, including big potential. Leismer development to reach up to 40,000 BOE/D is already being implemented.

The company also owns the Corner Field (capacity of 40,000 BBL/D) that could be developed or sold. Its Duvernay joint venture with Cenovus grows towards 6,000 BOE/D in 2025. These long-term opportunities are not yet priced in.

Tariffs

US rates: a short-term problem, perhaps even a better thing in the long term. In April 2025, the US announced a 10% tariff on certain Canadian energy products, including bitumen oil, the primary product of Athabasca Oil Corporation. It was part of a broader trade conflict over electric car subsidies. Some investors responded to this panic, and let's be honest, we had a selloff. Keep in mind that even if they are lifted, U.S. trade policy has proven volatile, so stocks like this may remain sensitive to headlines.

These tariffs can reduce the price by approximately $2-3 per barrel. Because Athabasca sells about 37 thousand daily, it could theoretically "cut" about 5-7% of profit - not nice, but far from a disaster. Especially knowing that their costs of doing business are very low and that free cash flow is still strong even at $60 per barrel.

Another thing is that Athabasca is not stuck in the US market. From 2024, the Trans Mountain Expansion (TMX) pipeline began to export heavy oil to Asia - China, Japan, and South Korea. Not only does it reduce the dependence on the US, but it also opens the door for higher prices where there are simply no tariffs.

Thus, from the investor's position, tariffs are more a temporary inconvenience than a real threat. And if tariffs stay, Athabasca has alternatives. If they disappear, we will have very clear catalysts for share price growth, and given this industry lobbying, it is very likely. Some investors see the risk here, but others see a chance. Emotions often create "sales" on the market - which is just a moment to enter.

Conclusion

The market often responds too much to short-term noises. Oil falls to $60, tariffs are announced, and investors are panicking. But those who look deeper see something else. Athabasca Oil Corporation is a classic case where the market overestimates risks and underestimates value.

This is not a speculative company that burns money and waits for the oil price to bounce. This is a company that is already profitable. With ~ $40 at a break-even point, more than 12% of free cash flow and 90 years reserves are a strong, efficient machine that generates money even under current conditions. It has small debt and excess cash of over 140 million, and in 2024 alone, it bought shares for more than $300 million.

Tariffs? Yes, they are unpleasant, but they do not change the essence of the business model. In addition, the TMX pipeline has already opened the door to Asian markets - where there are no rates and prices are often better. Athabasca has strategic freedom that many competitors would be jealous.

In the long run, here is what the value investors call "free options" - you can't pay for growth, but you still get it. Even if the oil remains at $60, Athabasca still works. If the price rises to $75, the shares can jump to a double-digit percentage.

My rating for it currently stands at BUY.

data.title: Tariffs Take Aim At Amazon: Navigating 'Liberation Day' Shock
data.description:
The Trump administration’s April 2025 “Liberation Day” tariffs represent a seismic shift in U.S. trade policy, imposing sweeping duties on imports from virtually all major trade partners. The plan introduced a baseline 10% tariff on all imports to the U.S. (effective April 5), with even higher rates on 57 targeted countries – including 20% on the EU, 26% on India, 24% on Japan, and a combined 125% on China when layered on prior China-specific tariffs.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), as a global e-commerce titan and a major cloud provider, faces a uniquely multifaceted exposure to this tariff storm. On the retail side, Amazon’s marketplace is deeply entwined with international supply chains: over 60% of its retail sales come from third-party sellers, many of whom source products from Asia and around the world. Amazon’s own first-party retail operations also import vast volumes of consumer goods into the U.S., from electronics to apparel, meaning the new import taxes could directly inflate its cost of goods sold.

Simultaneously, Amazon Web Services (AWS) – which leads the $200+ billion cloud industry – could become a collateral victim if trade partners respond with digital trade barriers or if economic uncertainty dampens corporate tech spending. Amazon’s extensive logistics and fulfillment infrastructure, spanning ports, warehouses, and delivery networks across continents, further illustrates its reliance on fluid global trade. The company operates over 1 billion square feet of fulfillment space worldwide (versus about 176 million for Walmart’s (WMT) e-commerce operations), a network designed to source and ship products efficiently across borders.

Tariff Mechanics & Amazon’s Operations

For Amazon’s 1P (first-party) retail business, this means many products it purchases from overseas manufacturers will suddenly cost more. In practice, Amazon faces a tough choice: absorb these added costs (hurting its profit margins), pass them onto consumers as higher prices (risking lower sales), or negotiate sharing the pain with suppliers. Amazon is already reportedly pressing some vendors to reduce their wholesale prices to offset tariffs. Its immense scale and buying power – it can leverage being many suppliers’ largest customer – may allow Amazon to claw back some concessions. However, vendors too are squeezed by the tariffs, and not all cost increases can be bargained away.

Shipping and fulfillment costs may also rise under tariff pressures. If certain foreign sourcing becomes uneconomical, Amazon might need to shift orders to alternative countries or domestic suppliers, potentially at higher base prices. Such reconfiguration isn’t instantaneous; as supply chain expert Marc Wulfraat notes, Amazon cannot easily “re-configure its supply chain meaningfully in short order” since it does not control many of its international suppliers (especially for third-party sellers). In the near term, Amazon could try to expedite imports before tariffs take full effect or draw down existing inventory stocked in the U.S. (indeed, Amazon reportedly had about 4 weeks of inventory on hand for many goods, which might delay the immediate impact). But once pre-tariff stockpiles run down by mid-quarter, Amazon’s operating costs on newly imported goods will reflect the tariff-driven inflation.

E-Commerce & Logistics Exposure

Amazon’s core e-commerce business – both the direct sales (1P) and the third-party marketplace (3P) – is highly exposed to tariff impacts, especially in discretionary product categories. We estimate a whopping ~87% of Amazon’s U.S. gross merchandise volume (GMV) is linked to products that could be hit by tariffs. This includes most non-essential items: electronics, apparel, toys, home goods, and more, which Amazon sources globally or hosts on its platform via foreign sellers. In contrast, essentials like groceries (a category where Amazon’s presence is smaller relative to total sales) have more domestic sourcing and thus face less direct tariff pressure.

The 3P marketplace dynamics add another layer. Third-party sellers (who account for ~60% of units sold on Amazon) are themselves importers or resellers of imported goods in many cases. These entrepreneurs and businesses will face the new tariffs when bringing inventory into the U.S., and their ability to manage these costs varies. Many 3P sellers from China, for instance, operate on thin margins and a 125% tariff would be devastating. For Amazon, one immediate concern is that higher costs will curtail third-party sellers’ activity, leading to lower unit volumes and possibly a reduced assortment on the platform.

Sellers may also cut their advertising spending on Amazon. A significant portion of Amazon’s ad revenue (from Sponsored Products, Sponsored Brands, etc.) comes from 3P sellers promoting their listings. If those sellers see sales slowing or margins shrinking, they are likely to pull back on ad bids to conserve budget. Analysts are indeed baking in lower advertising revenue growth for Amazon’s 3P segment due to tariffs. This is noteworthy because advertising is a high-margin business for Amazon; any softness there directly pressures operating profit.

Moreover, if prices across Amazon’s site inflate due to tariffs, demand elasticity could kick in – especially among price-sensitive consumers. Households with tighter budgets might reduce discretionary purchases on Amazon, or even cancel Prime memberships, if they feel they’re not getting the same value. Fewer Prime members would be an alarming trend for Amazon, as Prime not only drives frequent purchasing but also underpins services like Prime Video and Whole Foods integration. While a mass exodus is unlikely, even a slight uptick in churn or downtick in new Prime sign-ups could hurt Amazon’s long-term customer lifetime value.

Amazon’s vast fulfillment and logistics network also faces logistical and cost challenges. The company relies on a finely tuned flow of inventory from manufacturers worldwide into its fulfillment centers. With the tariff regime, Amazon may need to adjust its inventory strategies. We might see Amazon and its sellers front-loading inventory before higher tariffs hit (as happened in late 2018 ahead of scheduled tariff hikes, when importers rushed goods to U.S. ports).

AWS (Cloud Services) Vulnerabilities

Beyond general economic trends, retaliatory actions by foreign governments could directly target Amazon’s cloud business. Europe in particular has signaled that U.S. digital services are a point of leverage. The EU runs a large deficit in digital services trade with the U.S. (over €100 billion), and European officials have mused that “digital services could be Washington’s Achilles heel” in a trade confrontation. This suggests that European regulators might accelerate measures that had already been under discussion – for instance, digital services taxes (DSTs) or new rules under the EU’s Digital Markets Act – which could impose extra costs or constraints on AWS. A digital services tax, even a small percentage on revenues, would effectively act like a tariff on cloud services revenue in those countries.

Europe previously put DST plans on hold in favor of OECD negotiations, but the tariff clash could revive those efforts. Additionally, Europe or other regions could enforce stricter data localization mandates requiring that data of their citizens be stored and processed locally. AWS does build local data centers (it has AWS Europe regions in countries like Ireland, Germany, and France), but more onerous localization rules could force AWS to invest in even more infrastructure per country, raising costs. Countries could also push back on data transfers, citing the need to “shield” local data from an American company if trade relations sour – a non-tariff barrier that might complicate AWS’s unified cloud network.

Another vulnerability is hardware and infrastructure costs for AWS. While cloud services themselves aren’t imports, AWS’s data centers are built from vast amounts of servers, networking gear, and other hardware – much of which is manufactured in Asia. Tariffs on Chinese high-tech components or semiconductor products could raise AWS’s capital expenditures. If, say, certain server components now carry a 10–15% import tax, equipping new data centers or expanding existing ones becomes more expensive for Amazon. This could either compress AWS’s profit margins or prompt AWS to raise prices down the line (though cloud pricing is fiercely competitive, making price hikes difficult). There is also a risk of supply chain delays for data center equipment if trade tensions disrupt electronics supply lines. Any slowdown in AWS infrastructure rollout could hurt its ability to meet customer demand timely, though so far, Amazon has indicated it can manage capacity well.

Competition & Regulatory Pressure

In the U.S., Amazon’s main retail rival Walmart could prove relatively resilient during the tariff turbulence – and perhaps even capitalize on it. Walmart’s supply chain has a huge domestic component: it has thousands of stores that function as both sales points and distribution nodes, and over half of Walmart’s product volume (especially groceries and essentials) is sourced and sold locally via its stores. If consumers become more price-sensitive, Walmart’s “Everyday Low Price” promise and ability to offer in-store pickup for online orders could attract value-conscious shoppers. The net effect is that competition between Amazon and Walmart could intensify, with each leaning on their strengths (Amazon on selection and Prime convenience, Walmart on price and local presence) to retain customers amid cost pressures.

Looking globally, Amazon must watch local e-commerce upstarts in emerging markets, too. In India, for example, Amazon and Walmart-owned Flipkart dominate, but both rely on some imported goods (electronics, etc.). The Indian government, which has its own history of protectionist policies, might respond sympathetically to U.S. tariffs by promoting “Make in India” and favoring indigenous platforms or stricter e-commerce FDI rules that hamper Amazon. Similarly, in Latin America, MercadoLibre (MELI) could play up its regional roots to outcompete Amazon if U.S.-Latin trade friction increases.

Investor Implications

Tariffs have introduced fresh uncertainty into Amazon’s recovery trajectory and could translate into higher costs of goods by a few billion dollars. As a result, management may adopt a cautious tone in upcoming guidance for Q2 and full-year 2025, factoring in potential trade negotiation outcomes. This caution has already rattled investor sentiment, with the stock initially dipping amid concerns about slower top-line growth and tighter profit ranges.

Profitability remains the focal point. While Amazon’s margins have been improving since 2023, tariffs could compress retail margins even as AWS and advertising lift the overall mix. The scale of margin impact depends on whether Amazon absorbs most tariff costs or passes them along to customers. A decision to maintain low prices could dampen near-term profits but reinforce customer loyalty; conversely, broad price hikes might preserve margins while risking volume softness. Even so, existing warehouse automation, corporate headcount reductions, and other efficiencies offer a buffer, and I still project Amazon’s EBIT margin landing in the low-to-mid 11% range for 2025.

Conclusion

The April 2025 “Liberation Day” tariffs present a significant challenge for Amazon, impacting its supply chains, retail margins, and AWS growth, while potentially disrupting logistics efficiency and increasing consumer prices; however, Amazon’s vast scale, diversified operations, and adaptability position it uniquely to mitigate these pressures. Short-term tactical responses—including modest price increases, cost management, and supply chain optimization—will enable Amazon to maintain steady, albeit slower, growth and profitability amidst the trade tensions. Amazon’s handling of this situation will be closely observed as a gauge for broader e-commerce inflation and digital market friction, with expectations generally optimistic given its historical resilience. Investors may see temporary margin pressure and restrained international expansion, yet Amazon’s core franchises and robust financial strength ensure its long-term trajectory remains solid, allowing it to potentially emerge stronger when trade conditions eventually normalize.

data.title: Bellway: Buy For The Growth Story, Avoid For The Lack Of Passive Income
data.description:
Like many of its housebuilding peers, Bellway (BLWYY) (OTCPK:BLWYF) stock has seen its share price tumble since the Autumn Budget, and has found it difficult to recover since amidst the market's volatility surrounding President Trump's tariffs. With the stock now trading below its book value, Bellway may be worth taking a look at for its growth story, rather than its dividends.

Brick by Brick

Bellway reported a rather encouraging set of figures in its interim results. Completions continued their upward momentum (+11.9%) to 4,577, and was complemented by the uptick in its net private sales rate (0.51 vs 0.43), as well as the 0.4% increase in the Average Selling Price [ASP] to £310,581. As a consequence, revenue was up a healthy 12.3% to £1.43B.

This hardy increase flowed through to underlying gross profit, which rose 11.1% to £234MM, but was offset slightly by the 17bps decrease in underlying gross margin to c.16.4% due to residual cost inflation, longer construction timelines, and customer incentives. Nonetheless, the builder's underlying EBIT margin was steady at c.11.0% with underlying EBIT 11.9% higher at £157MM.

Underlying net finance expense was unchanged from the year before, at £18MM. Paired with a tax rate that was also steady, Bellway's underlying profit after tax was 11.7% better, at £107MM. As a result, underlying diluted EPS also jumped 11.9% to 89.6p. Alongside this, the board lifted its interim DPS by a whopping 31.3% to 21.0p, much to the music of shareholder's ears.

But most promisingly, management reiterated its guidance to build 8,500 homes in FY25. To sweeten the deal, it even set a target to build another c.9,200 homes by FY26, as the company lines up more capacity from its landbank. This should, therefore, lead to better returns for shareholders, as evidenced by the large leap in dividends recently.

To reflect this, Bellway's order book grew yet again, to a total of 5,582 homes － its highest since H1 '23. And if its guidance is anything to go by, this should continue to build over the coming years, with the scope of returning to 2022 levels as mortgage rates drop over time. In fact, the group is set to open 30 more outlets in H2, translating to greater capacity to build more homes.

Building Its Way Up the Ladder

That being said, the main reason why Bellway is able to achieve such a rapid rate of growth as compared to some of its other peers like Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODY) (OTCPK:TWODF) and Persimmon (OTCPK:PSMMY) (OTCPK:PSMMF), is because it's one of the main beneficiaries of the government's new housing targets. This is due to the fact that the Newcastle-based firm is one of the bigger builders of family homes.

For context, the new Labour government has promised to shake up the UK's housing planning system that has been ridden with thick layers of red tape over the years. These include fast tracking the process of approving urban brownfield sites, mandatory housing targets, and the reintroduction of 5-year land supply rules, amongst many others.

While these rules have yet to take full effect, Bellway has, nonetheless, been one of the few housebuilders to have seen a rampant spike in its order book. This is partly because of the expansion of its sales network through its strong outlet opening programme, with financial initiatives such as cashbacks and a more competitive ASP resonating well with buyers.

Another positive for Bellway is the fact that a large portion of its landbank has received detailed planning consent. This means that it has a significant advantage in terms of future development potential, as it reduces the time and uncertainty associated that many of its other competitors have when it comes to building new homes and bringing them to market.

Combine the above with the fact that build activity has been ramping up, and one could make an argument that Bellway has got a sizeable tailwind blowing in its favour. This is reflected in the tick up in brick deliveries since the Labour government was elected, as it's often a leading indicator for completions. This should spell good news that the constructor is likely to hit its FY25 guidance.

Clearing the Way

The stock was previously trading at 22 times its forward earnings late last year, with much of the growth looking to have been priced in. However, the recent drop does look to have presented a lucrative buying opportunity, especially if the underlying thesis and guidance don't change over the medium term, with the stock now trading at a mere 12 times its forward earnings.

That is, of course, easier said than done given the volatile tariff dynamic at play. There's now a very real risk that a global trade war could unfold and trigger a recession into motion. After all, bank stocks, which are often a leading indicator for the housing market, have been falling from grace as investors alike begin to price in lower borrowing as a result of a recession.

The flip side, however, is that gilt yields have also fallen from their highs to touch YTD lows. Gilt yields are often a leading indicator for mortgage rates. So, if yields can fall further, we imagine lenders will follow suit with rate cuts. And if these do end up becoming big enough, they could stimulate the housing market further, and offset any supposed impact from tariffs.

And although there have been some downward revisions from Chancellor Rachel Reeves's housing targets, as she now only forecasts a gradual increase to achieve a build rate of 305k houses/yr by the end of parliament in 2029, it's still an improvement from the rates seen over the past few years. As such, we have conviction that Bellway would stand to benefit from this still.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests upside of c.40.1%, with a forward dividend yield of c.2.8%. This is derived by comparing Bellway's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model Bellway Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
EV/Underlying EBITDA (10% wt) 11.2 10.0 7.8 1,812p
P/E (10% wt) 15.8 15.3 14.5 2,139p
FP/E (25% wt) 12.0 16.7 10.8 2,595p
PEG (55% wt) 0.7 1.5 1.0 3,916p
Fair Value 3,200p

***As a reminder to our readers, Bellway's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:BWY). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios with an emphasis on forward-looking earnings multiples:

  • EV/EBITDA (10% wt);
  • P/E (10% wt);
  • Forward P/E (25% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (55% wt);

We prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations rather than past performance. Although, we include trailing ratios with lower weightings in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (OTC:WACC) and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. EV/EBITDA (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1 '25 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • Underlying EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Depreciation.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  2. P/E (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1 '25 results.
    • EPS is calculated from underlying diluted EPS on profit after tax, divided by diluted shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  3. FP/E (25% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections, if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1 '25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1 '25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS/100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from diluted EPS on FTM profit after tax, divided by projected diluted shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.
  4. FPEG (55% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS/TTM EPS) ^ (1/2.5 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price/TTM EPS)/CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Playtika: Fun 'N Games, Big Yield, Growth Catalysts, And Investors' Blinders
data.description:
Surely, you’ve heard the expression “the straw that broke the camel’s back.”

Wikipedia says this…

describes a minor or routine action that causes an unpredictably large and sudden reaction due to the cumulative effect of previous small actions.

There are many variations. Sometimes the object is a feather, a peppercorn, a hair or a melon… or even something non-specific as an ounce. The carrier-animal could also be a horse, a donkey, or a monkey.

Notice, though, what’s missing.

Think of every time you’ve heard that expression, or something similar. It’s used to describe or warn of something bad.

Is humanity so inherently pessimistic?

Have philosophers here ditched Yin-Yang (“complementary and at the same time opposing forces”). How about Newton’s third law (“For every action, there’s an equal and opposite reaction”).

I tried… but I could not come up with a memorable final-straw-like idiom that suggests something unexpectedly good.

The last drop of fuel that successfully launched the rocket… Nah. Physicists can probably identify that drop. But the idea fails poetically.

Maybe that’s why shares of Playtika Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:PLTK) look so dreadful right now.

I admit that when it comes to growth, nobody’s accusing PLTK of plagiarizing from Nvidia’s (NVDA) story.

And PLTK has shown itself to be pretty bad at playing the great guidance game. (We’ve seen negative surprises and downward revisions.)

But PLTK has an “A” Seeking Alpha Valuation Grade. How often do you see that nowadays for companies with good forward prospects! (See below.)

And it has an approximately 8% dividend yield. (That equated to an “A” Seeking Alpha Dividend Yield Sector Relative Grade.) And we see this despite the company being profitable, having a dividend to free cash flow payout ratio near 22%, and buying back stock.

Folks, if you hate 50+ P/Es… this is what value looks like, especially nowadays!

Naturally, there’s some baggage. (Surely you don’t expect a NVDA clone to sell for 9 times earnings!) I’ll get to that… and why it doesn’t discourage me.

But first, let’s understand what PLTK is about.

The First Four Letters of Playtika say it… This Company is About Mobile Games

Put the emphasis on “mobile.”

Don’t expect PLTK to cede major e-sports leagues, tournaments, or superstars.

The players are regular people, like you and me. (Surely, you’ve put something fun on your phone… That’s OK. It’ll be our secret.)

And I’ll bet you didn’t pay anything for the install, or even to start playing. (At least I hope you didn’t. These games are typically free to install.)

Now, PLTK isn’t a non-profit charity. It needs to make money. That’s where IAP (in-app purchases) comes in.

At some point, as you play, you get stuck.

Sometimes, you find yourself blocked from advancing to a higher level. You’ll then have to pay to do so.

Other times, you find that further progress, while theoretically possible, is too darned hard. So, you decide to purchase some sort of tool, or superpower, that helps you to keep going.

As contemporary leisure goes, these purchases tend to be very painless… many cost $5.99 or less.

Cheap entertainment… This is a nice market to be in.

Now, if you peruse the Apple App Store or the Google Play Store, you’ll find an insane number of available titles within each game category/genre. Undoubtedly, there are many also-rans in this business. But…

PLTK’s Game Portfolio is Well Represented At or Near the Tops of Key Categories

Since it began in 2010, still-founder-led PLTK has grown a lot. It develops games. But it acquires most of them.

Here’s where PLTK stands at present.

Starting from the upper left of this latter image and moving clockwise, the relevant categories/genres are…. Bingo & Board Games; Social Casino; Casual & Puzzle; Card games; Casual & Puzzle; and Poker.

Here are historic trends in key monetization-related metrics.

(In the deck’s glossary, PLTK defines Daily Paying Conversion as Daily Paying Users divided by Daily Active Users.)

A Cold Streak

I’m sure you noticed the post-2020 flattening in Daily Paying Users.

My interpretation of what happened (i.e., my words, not management’s) boils down to growing pains.

Let’s look more closely at the company’s acquisitions.

That’s a lot!

This isn’t Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B). The latter is a financial buyer that leaves each purchased unit relatively free to operate autonomously.

PLTK buys games to operate them.

And while games are the relaxation for players, they require a lot of managerial elbow grease. How do you keep games fresh while not messing up features players love? And how do you persuade players to keep going with IAPs.

Here’s a portion of PLTK’s playbook.

If you play mobile games, you’ll probably recognize these. For the uninitiated, these are events staged by games to generate further interest (and, hopefully, more IAPs).

These require considerable thought and planning. So as of 12/31/24, about 75% of PLTK employees were involved in research & development (10-K, page 12). Although most of the games were acquired rather than internally developed, this R&D is important “to consistently introduce updates and enhancements to (the) games”.

PLTK is also expert at “Live Operations Services” (10-K pages 9-10). This refers to…

the ongoing management and optimization of a game after its initial launch. LiveOps involves monitoring performance and behavior, updating content, and responding to data and player feedback to enhance the gaming experience. The goal of LiveOps is to make games more dynamic and engaging, which in turn boosts player retention and revenue opportunities.

So obviously, it takes a lot to manage a collection of games.

This is especially important as games age. So, given the number of games, and PLTK’s size, this makes for a big to-do list.

But it seems, PLTK lost sight of some important strategic balances it needed to do.

Slotomania, for example, one of PLTK’s older games, has been struggling lately. That’s due to what CFO Craig Abrahams described (on the 2/27/25 earnings call) as “game economy issues.”

Add given ever-resent competition, and the result is less than great.

Embarking on a “Return to Growth”

That’s how C.E.O. Robert Antokol described the company’s current priority on the 2/27/25 earnings call.

Here’s a sense of what this is about…

  • Managing the Games as a Portfolio

One thing, in particular, management talks about is a newly adopted “portfolio approach.”

PLTK now recognizes it can’t manage each game as a singular entity. That has led to suboptimal strategic resource allocation.

Here’s how Abrahams described the portfolio approach on the earnings call:

  • Games with scale and growth potential: “invest significantly to drive further expansion.”
  • Games with scale but limited growth potential: defend “market share through targeted spending.”
  • Games lacking scale but having promising growth potential: will “receive outsized investments.”

That enhances the likelihood that PLTK will give each game the focus in needs based on its role within the portfolio.

This should prevent PLTK from taking its eye off the ball. So, presumably, what we saw with Slotomania won’t recur. Nor should anything like that happen with any other game.

  • Building PLTK’s Direct to Consumer (DTC) Efforts

We usually think of getting games through an app store, such as those maintained by Apple and Google.

But it doesn’t have to be that way. Companies like PLTK can set up their own web portals for individual games.

For example, one can play PLTK’s Bingo Blitz game by downloading it from an app store. Or one can play it directly on the Bingo Blitz web site PLTK established. The latter is DTC.

I tried it both ways. The look, feel, and game play was the same.

But DTC gives PLTK some strong advantages.

It helps the company foster player engagement and loyalty… PLTK can collect more detailed data and more effectively customize rewards, promotions and IAP bundles based on what it knows about its customers

It’s also good for the bottom line. PLTK need not pay the 30% commissions on IAPs demanded by the App Stores.

With DTC, it need not worry about what changing App-Store privacy dictates might impact its data-collection ability.

And given controversies that are brewing over gambling-oriented games, PLTK would be free, if necessary, to fine tune its own policies if it comes to believe app stores start going overboard. (That’s not the case now, but if things change, it would serve PLTK well to have a Plan B.)

DTC is a large (27.2% of 2024 revenues) and growing area for PLTK.

  • The 11/30/24 SuperPlay Acquisition

This is the last drop of fuel that…. No, let’s instead say the straw that fixed… Aw heck, let’s just call it an ostensibly mundane acquisition, one among many, that, when combined with the rest of PLTK, is likely to serve as a major positive catalyst.

The deal isn’t cheap for a company PLTK’s size. The upfront cost was $700 million. Depending on how SuperPlay performs between 2025 and 2027, the price could rise by another $1.25 billion.

Accordingly, it’s worth a closer look…

SuperPlay and its Impact on PLTK

SuperPlay was founded in 2019 by two former PLTK employees and another industry veteran. According to Gamelight, this business…

has quickly emerged as a powerhouse in the mobile gaming space. Its top titles, Dice Dreams and Domino Dreams, have both experienced impressive user growth.

As of August 2024, Dice Dreams and Domino Dreams had a combined 1.7 million daily active users, demonstrating SuperPlay’s ability to capture and maintain player engagement on a global scale. With these hit titles and two more games in development, Playtika is acquiring not just successful games but also an expert team with a talent for creating hit games.

Note that last sentence. The deal will boost PLTK’s in-house game development capabilities. (This is important. In the wake of Slotomania’s slump, PLTK is developing something new for this important genre.)

This is not to say PLTK will stop acquiring. But having a strong in-house capability has to be a plus.

SuperPlay already has two big hits on the charts, Dice Dreams and Domino Dreams.

Notice the right hand-side of the above slide. Solitaire is an important game genre. And Disney is a powerful brand. So the prospective Disney Solitaire launch looks very promising.

Meanwhile, SuperPlay’s established titles became immediate difference-makers for PLTK’s game portfolio.

So given the qualitative impact (strengthening PLTK’s internal development capabilities) and its quantitative impact (needle-moving titles), I’ll say, SuperPlay isn’t just an acquisition… It’s an ACQUISITION!

The Numbers

The historic growth rates reflect that impact of the above-discussed cold streak.

(I compare PLTK to medians among companies in the Interactive Home Entertainment industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Because PLTK is small, I also compare it to the SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (SPSM). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

The forward growth expectations reflect the reality that the company’s “Return to Growth” program is not going to do what it needs to do overnight.

Here are the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

We see again in the EPS trends that “Return to Growth” will take time.

Here are the core fundamentals.

I’ll start by commenting on the NM (Not Meaningful) entries you see above. These reflect the company having negative net worth.

Don’t panic.

That’s not coming from money-losing operations. Return on Assets actually shows the company to be ok in this regard.

The negative net worth comes from transactions booked before PLTK’s 1/15/21 IPO.

Ideally, we’d like to think that a company will be initially sent out into the public markets with a balance sheet that looks as strong as possible. In reality, that often does not happen.

Page 88 of the 12/18/20 S-1 document issued in connection with the IPO stated:

Net cash flows used in financing activities for the nine months ended September 30, 2019 included borrowings on our Bridge Facility of $2.6 billion offset by a distribution to our shareholder of $2.4 billion and $89.6 million in repayments on our short-term bank credit.

(Emphasis supplied.)

What can I say, except welcome to the world of IPOs!

I don’t love this. But I’m not King of the World, so I can’t change it.

We do see, however, that debt is high, and interest coverage is relatively low.

The latter is perfectly adequate from my back-in-the-day perspective as a high yield bond mutual fund manager. (I tolerated 1.00, accepted 1.25, and probably would have sold my soul to Michael Milken for 2.00. )

But equity investors prefer to see higher ratios.

I get that. And I, too, given my current equities’ vantage point, would like to see better from PLTK in the future.

But I won’t argue with borrowing for productive purposes. And to me, PLTK’s acquisitions, especially the SuperPlay purchase, look to be quite productive.

Here are the Financial Statement Highlights.

I’ve already commented on the most eye-catching balance-sheet-related elements.

Note, though, the dividends.

Forget the pre-2021 payouts… that was private company stuff that’s not relevant to public-markets shareholders (except to the extent we might wish the equity account would be positive).

The 2024 figure is interesting. The dividend, first declared on 2/26/24, is part of what Antokol describes as a strategic effort (that also includes share buybacks) to return capital to shareholders.

The payout, 22.8% of Cash from Operations (see Financial Statement Highlights above) or 34.5% of estimated FY1 Cash Flow is reasonable.

Risks

I have to start with the human element.

PLTK is headquartered in Israel. Antokol addressed that, including a discussion of contingency plans, early on the first earnings call after the initial 10/7/23 terrorist attack. You can see his remarks here.

With regard to mundane business matters…

We’ve already seen the major risk factor for a company like this play out… the cold streak… the potential for some games to get stale and succumb to competition. The “Return to Growth” effort is designed to address this.

As noted, there’s a lot of debt. But I believe that capital is being productively used. And management’s capital allocation decisions reflect its confidence in PLTK’s financial position.

There are two other topics I’d like to discuss that I don’t see being addressed in the investment world.

  • Can IAP’s Drain Regular Players?

It’s at least theoretically possible that players might overspend. That could make them slash playing time or quit altogether.

As I mentioned above, IAPs tend to be very small ticket. So, I think it would take unusual… let’s be polite and say, “devotion” before IAPs become troublesome.

If you research game reviews online, as I did, you’ll find considerable devotion, but also complaints about IAPs being a “money grab.”

Now, I presume you’re no stranger to social media reviews of everything and anything on a variety of platforms. And undoubtedly, you've understood many should be taken with a grain of salt… there are malcontents everywhere.

But analytic prudence makes me hesitate to dismiss such complaints out of hand… i.e., absent objective data.

Suffice it to say, PLTKs numbers can be seen as giving us objective data. This company’s has about $2.5 billion in revenues. That, and the usage statistics shown above, suggests the overwhelming majority of users feel they’re getting good value for what they spend.

That still doesn’t mean everything is always perfect.

I believe eyes-off-the-ball mismatches between what players wanted to spend and what PLTK wanted to receive contributed to the cold streak.

With PLTK’s portfolio approach now keeping it more constructively focused, I expect it to alleviate any future spending issues before any devolve into crises.

That’s where Live Operations Services comes to the fore.

PLTK can use this to monitor play and spending. And where needed, this can help PLTK adjust its strategic content layers in ways that better balance between PLTK’s desire to monetize and players’ desires to keep spending under control.

I assume such efforts will fix Slotomania and help PLTK recover from its cold streak

  • Also, There’s Another Monetization Tool… if PLTK Decides it Wants to Use it

I’m talking about in-game advertising.

This, along with increasing ads in previously ad-free streaming TV, is growing. I discussed this previously in connection with The Trade Desk (TTD), AppLovin (APP) and Unity Software (U).

At present, PLTK is not interested. When asked about in-game ads during the latest earnings call, Abrahams answered:

We've traditionally focused on in-app purchases. We believe that our expertise in live operations allows us to have better monetization and better long-term retention as a result of that focus. And while we do have some games that do have in-app advertising, we don't see that as a driver for us

So, noted!

But that’s the situation now. Who knows what may happen in the future if circumstances change.

Just be aware that if player resistance to IAPs were to reach a level PLTK can’t address with its Live Operations Services, it could introduce in-game ads

In streaming, it’s working for Netflix (NFLX) and others. It could, if need be, work for PLTK too.

What to do About PLTK Stock

At the top of this article, I suggested PKLTK is a value play.

As noted there, it has a Seeking Alpha “A” Value Grade. Beyond that, here are my preferred metrics.

Those numbers suggest that the Street is obsessing on the company’s recent performance. The latest rotten guidance-game outcome is acting almost like a pair of horse blinders.

If that’s what investors want to do, then so be it. But allowing one’s self to be blinded by the guidance game leads to one missing a lot… a heck of a lot, actually.

The Street is acting as if past performance does determine future outcomes. But it doesn’t. Things can change. And they often do.

I don’t think the Street is paying serious attention to the “Return to Growth” strategy. (I wonder if they’d take it more seriously if PLTK would have gotten better public-relations advice and come up with a less-corny label. Oh, well…)

Most notably, it doesn’t look like Mr. Market appreciates the potential impact of the SuperPlay acquisition.

Don’t be surprised if numbers start coming in above expectations as we approach 2026. (And suppose PLTK changes its mind about advertising and decides that while it doesn’t have to chase it, a new revenue stream could still be nice.)

Here’s the price chart.

Ugh!

What a mess.

The price trend, the 10- and 50-day exponential moving average, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF), Chaikin Oscillator (CO)… Too bad this chart is electronic. If it were printed on paper, at least we could expect it to eventually biodegrade.

Let’s go back to the valuation numbers.

As they are, they look quite promising.

But frankly, I think PLTK can exceed the Proj. 3-5Y EPS Gr. If that happens, that will make the already low PEG FWD look even more attractive.

I don’t expect PLTK to take off right away. But then, with all the problems with which the general market is wrestling, that doesn’t necessarily put PLTK at a disadvantage. Moreover…

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see PLTK as likely outperforming the broader market, probably over a year or so rather than over the next quarter. But with such a high and well covered dividend yield, and the lean valuations, we can afford to be patient.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating PLTK as a “Buy.”

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: CVR Partners: The Nitrogen Fertilizer Market Looking Ripe For An Upswing
data.description:
Introduction

The basic material sector has been one of the lagging sectors in the US market for over the last 12 months. The overall market favored sectors like technologies and communication services and the old economy sector, like basic materials, lagged behind the overall market. Being one of the companies within the basic material sector, CVR Partners (NYSE:UAN) witnessed similar price movement with marginal outperformance against the basic material sector but an underperformance against the broader market index (S&P 500).

However, I believe the company is set to deliver market-beating returns going forward, driven by favorable demand-supply dynamics in the nitrogen fertilizer market. Although the valuation ratio is a little stretched, I believe it's decent enough to issue a buy rating on the stock, especially given the upside opportunity it can have if fertilizer prices increase going forward.

4Q24 earnings recap

Back in February, CVR Partners came out with a decent set of numbers. Its topline came in at $139.56 million or a -1.5% decline YoY, while the bottom line had been quite impressive, growing ~83% YoY to $1.73. Moreover, the company also announced a quarterly cash distribution of $1.75 per common unit, so as a result, the full-year 2024 cash distribution ended up at $6.76 per common unit.

Ammonia sales for 4Q24 were in line YoY; however, UAN sales volumes were down ~3%, partially due to adverse weather conditions during the fall application. Similarly, pricing for ammonia stood strong with a 3% increase YoY, while UAN prices declined ~5% YoY due to the same weather-related impact. Even though weather conditions were not favorable in 4Q24, the demand for nitrogen fertilizer was good, leading to somewhat better pricing in 4Q24 compared to the previous quarter. This strength in pricing continued during 2025.

Buoyant market conditions to support earnings

I believe the nitrogen fertilizer market is quite buoyant given the recent rally in corn prices and higher acreage for corn. Meanwhile, supply-related bottlenecks related to China’s export ban for fertilizers and a general lower level of inventory at dealers should also contribute to a beneficial market dynamic for fertilizer producers in the US.

Starting off with the demand side of the equation, 2024 ended with a strong fall application season resulting in good demand for fertilizers, and now the spring season is anticipated to be good also. The rise in prices of corn is a good incentive for farmers to sow these crops for a better yield. As per CoBank, the outperformance of corn prices over other crops should lead to a ~4.2% YoY higher acreage of corn. Corn is a major consumer of nitrogen-based fertilizer, with the grain alone accounting for 50% of the nitrogen-based fertilizer demand in the USA. Hence, a higher acreage of corn should naturally benefit demand for nitrogen-based fertilizer in 2025.

In addition to this, 2025 is also going to be a good year for farm income, primarily due to the government assistance flowing in through the American Relief Act 2025. The direct transfer from the government to farmers for 2025 is going to be ~$42.4 billion, up ~$33.1 billion from a year ago, primarily due to increased funding to the USDA under the American Relief Act 2025. This level of government assistance to the farm sector was previously only provided in the year 2020. The increased farm income in 2025 should find its way in the form of higher spending capabilities of farmers.

That being said, for the demand side of the equation, the supply side of the equation is equally in favor of fertilizer producers. The global supply of nitrogen fertilizers has been tight, with Europe struggling with the higher cost of raw materials such as natural gas, and China having an export ban on key fertilizers such as Urea.

In addition to the tight global supply of fertilizers, the inventory levels in the network are at a lower level than the average, primarily due to reduced imports. These lower inventory levels can act as a fuel to fire if any kind of supply bottleneck emerges in the foreseeable future. Given robust planned corn acreage during the 2025 spring season, I believe a strong surge in demand could trigger a minor shortage of nitrogen fertilizer in the US market.

Insider buying instill confidence in CVR

Carl Icahn, through its investment companies and its subsidiaries, is one of the largest shareholders of CVR Partners, with a ~38% stake in the company. Usually, it is insiders who get to know the developments in a company first than the common investors like us, so insider buying is a signal to watch out for.

In the case of CVR Partners, Carl Icahn, through its various investment companies, is gradually raising its stake in the company. Since the November 2024, he has been buying a stake in the company at regular intervals, acquiring more than 200K shares in the company to date.

Valuation

CVR Partners is currently trading at a 12x TTM price-to-book ratio, as compared to a 14x sector median. While the PE ratio is decent, CVR’s EPS is too volatile. Consider the years 2023 and 2022; during this period, CVR had an EPS of $16.3 and $27, respectively. If we consider these EPS, the PE ratio would be somewhere around 3-4x, but those earnings are not feasible unless there is a steep rise in fertilizer prices.

A better gauge of valuation could be price-to-book. Current price to tangible book value stands at ~2.5x, which is quite elevated when compared to peers like Nutrien (NTR) (~1x P/B) and LSB Industries (LXU) (~0.8x P/B). However, CVR’s P/B is comparable to another peer, CF Industries (CF) (2.4x P/B).

However, CVR has much better profitability and return ratio compared to its peers and its ratio is much more comparable to CF industries, which also have 2.4x Price to book but slightly better ratios.

Conclusion

I believe the US fertilizer market is ripe for a major swing in favor of producers like CVR Partners, given the supply-demand dynamics. Moreover, the consistent insider buying gives even more confidence in the company’s prospects going forward. Although valuation is a little elevated, I believe it is decent enough to initiate a buy rating, especially given the near-term possibility of fertilizer price increases.

Risk

Raw material price risk

CVR’s primary raw materials include natural gas and petcoke. A steep rise in the domestic price of these raw materials can have an adverse impact on the business and profit margins of the company.

Adverse weather event impacting agriculture

In the event of adverse weather events, usage of fertilizers tends to drop. Any unforeseen weather event can impact the demand as well as prices of fertilizer in the short term.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: YPF: The Most Tangible Opportunity For Argentine Equity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The historic Argentine company is at an inflection point, consolidating its position as the country's leading integrated oil company. It is delivering strong results in operational efficiency and profitability, underpinned by a long-term strategy focused on shale development and export growth. Its importance in the development of Vaca Muerta is key, not only for the possibility of energy self-sufficiency, but also for the export potential it represents for the country.

The execution of the "4x4 Plan", the construction of the VMOS pipeline, and the preliminary agreements to export LNG are important points that position the company as the central axis of the new national energy agenda.

Although the macroeconomic environment remains complex and legal risks still generate uncertainty, I consider that YPF (NYSE:YPF) has revealed the ability to adapt: it improved margins, reduced relative leverage and managed to fund itself at competitive interest rates. Analyzing the valuation, the stock operates at strong discounts to the global sector, offering an attractive entry point for investors with a medium to long-term horizon. At the same time, its consolidation in operational and financial terms strengthens its flexibility in the face of external shocks and local volatility.

For all these reasons, I consider the risk-return ratio favorable, and I therefore recommend buying. YPF's operational dynamism, projected growth in production and exports, and the opportunity for significant appreciation if the regulatory framework normalizes make it a significant opportunity within the emerging asset market.

Overview

YPF S.A. is Argentina's biggest integrated energy company, which operates in hydrocarbons exploration, production, refining, and commercialization. The company maintains its leadership in the local oil and gas market and also in refining activities. Its role in the country's energy agenda not only strengthens the local markets but also export opportunities.

The macroeconomic conditions in which YPF operates persist as challenging. Argentina is implementing a structural adjustment program under the initiatives of the Milei administration. These adjustments include tax reforms, the gradual removal of energy subsidies, tariff realignment with the currency, and liberalized free-market pricing systems. In an attempt to restore the economic equilibrium and encourage investment, these measures, on the other hand, create short-term pressures: consumption is low, inflation is high, and changes in policy are frequent. This severely affects the energy industry due to its controlled prices, limited access to foreign currencies, and production incentives.

From this perspective, the most essential and with the greatest prospect of the company is located in Vaca Muerta, which is among the largest reserves of shale oil and gas in the world. YPF leads the development of this formation, representing nearly 40% of the country's unconventional production.

The company's current strategic plan aims to multiply its value by four in the next 4 years ("4x4 Plan"), supported by intensive investments in upstream and key infrastructure projects to enable exports. The construction of the Vaca Muerta Sur pipeline (VMOS), with an estimated capacity of 390,000 barrels per day (KBOE/D), and the agreements for future export of LNG to Asia, position YPF at the center of the national energy strategy. According to data from the government and the company itself, in 2025 alone it is expected to invest more than $3 billion in the development of unconventional gas.

Throughout 2025, YPF has consolidated signs of strategic progress that reinforce this expansion. In January, it successfully placed $1.1 billion in bonds at a rate of 8.5%, intended to repurchase short-term debt and improve its financial profile. A few weeks later, it signed a memorandum with Indian companies for the future export of LNG, in line with its objective of positioning itself as a regional energy supplier.

Also, the formation of a conglomerate of seven oil companies to advance with the VMOS pipeline reinforces its purpose to turn Argentina into an export hub. But complex fronts moreover persist, such as the 2012 expropriation litigation, which continues its course in US courts and represents an important potential liability for the State and for the company's balance sheet.

Altogether, YPF is operating in 2025 with a determined strategy, supporting in the development of unconventional resources and the internationalization of its energy business, as stated by its CEO, Horacio Marín, on April 11th in Freedom Hall on the 7th floor of the NYSE, under the phrase: "Delivering the full potential of Vaca Muerta Argentina". However, it is still exposed to the macroeconomic, judicial, and political risks that characterize the Argentine market.

4Q 2024 and FY 2024

YPF finished FY 2024 with improvements in its results, both in operating and financial terms, indicating that it is in a period of expansion linked to production and efficiency in unconventional developments.

Net revenues were $19.3 billion in FY 2024 (+11% YoY), explained by the rise in local fuel prices and growth in oil exports. In Q4 2024, net revenues fell 10%, to $4.75 billion, due to lower seasonal gas sales and the reduction of domestic fuel prices.

Adjusted EBITDA was $4.6 billion in FY 2024 (+15% YoY), and EBITDA margin was 24.2% in 2024 compared to 23.4% in 2023, and in Q4 2024 it reached $839 million (-39% YoY) and the margin was 17.6%.

Net income was positive: $2.4 billion in 2024, compared to a loss of $1.3 billion in 2023, and in Q4 2024, it had a loss of $284 million, contrasted to a profit of $1.48 billion in Q4 2023.

CAPEX reached $5.04 billion (-5% YoY), in line with YPF's target ($5 billion): 63.5% of this corresponded to non-conventionals investments, while FCF for 2024 was negative, at $760 million, 3% higher than 2023 and net debt ended FY 2024 at $7.43 billion, (+9% YoY), but the net debt / EBITDA ratio decreased to 1.6x compared to 1.7x in 2023.

At an operational level, YPF achieved a total production of 536,000 barrels equivalent per day (KBOE/D), representing a 4% YoY rise.

This increase was mainly explained by Vaca Muerta, where shale oil production increased by 21% and shale gas by 11%, leading to unconventional developments now representing 53% of the total produced by the company.

Valuation

The company has a low valuation relative to its energy sector peers, despite solid improvements in its fundamentals. The P/E (FWD) is 5.11x, a 46.5% discount to the average of the 17 largest companies in the sector (9.56x) and 44.2% compared to the top seven companies in the integrated oil and gas sector (9.16x).

Meanwhile, the EV/EBITDA (FWD) is located at 3.66x, 26.1% below the sector average (4.95x) and 10.9% below the top 7 (4.11x). Furthermore, the P/E ratio is 0.80x: although it is still below the sector (1.21x) and larger companies (1.1x), it is double its historical average of 0.48x, which allows me to observe an equity revaluation in line with improved return expectations.

These compressed multiples contrast with solid operating performance. EBITDA grew 261% YoY and EBIT 913.5%, with additional growth projections of 16.7% and 58.7%, respectively. The net margin (11.6%) already exceeds the sector average (9.1%), ROE has soared to 21.6%, more than double that of the sector (10.6%), and ROE (FWD) stands at 16.46%, well above expectations for the seven largest companies in the sector, supported by improved operating leverage and efficient use of capital. Operating cash generation grew 215% YoY, and the Price/Cash Flow (FWD) of 2.57x implies a strong discount with respect to the sector (4.52x).

However, challenges persist: FCF margin remains negative (-6.05%) due to an aggressive CAPEX policy (30.1% of sales), well above the sector (17.3%), aimed at expanding production, mainly in Vaca Muerta.

Added to this are risks such as potential tariffs or external restrictions, along with local political and judicial uncertainty, which partly explain the valuation penalty. Even in this scenario, I believe that the strong growth in revenue (+226% YoY), ROA (6.9%), and tangible book value (+66% YoY) supports a thesis of sustained operational recovery.

In the integrated analysis of YPF's indicators, I must highlight that it combines low multiples with improving fundamentals, strong operating growth, and expanding returns, suggesting high potential for revaluation if the macro and regulatory context stabilizes.

Risks

YPF faces a complex environment in which multiple sources of risk converge and influence its future performance. At the local level, the political transition in Argentina, with a government promoting accelerated economic liberalization, generates short-term uncertainty. While a more investment-friendly framework is being sought, the process entails a profound reconfiguration of subsidies, tariffs, and controls that could generate disruptions in the energy chain, social tensions, and regulatory volatility. In this context, any unexpected adjustment in internal fuel prices or changes in the sector's tax structure could affect demand, operating costs, or refining margins.

Additionally, Argentina's macroeconomic fragility must be taken into account: the scarcity of Central Bank reserves, the distortion in relative prices, and the need to implement fiscal corrections generate pressure on purchasing power, make imported inputs more expensive, and make financial planning difficult. In addition, the exchange gap and restrictions on currency transfers continue to be an obstacle for YPF's international operations, which must import equipment, pay foreign debt or distribute dividends.

Internationally, the energy landscape is increasingly volatile. The conflict in the Middle East, tensions in the Red Sea, and expectations regarding Chinese demand are generating uncertainty about crude oil prices in the coming months. Any fall in the Brent price would affect YPF's export revenues and deteriorate incentives to expand production at Vaca Muerta. Added to this are global financial conditions, with higher interest rates and greater risk aversion, which complicate access to capital in international markets, which could both delay project execution and increase the cost of debt.

Finally, the legal battle in New York remains a latent risk. Although the ruling is not yet final, the equity impact resulting from YPF's nationalization could influence investment decisions, market access, and the perception of country-company risk in the immediate future.

In short, the company is facing a period of great potential, but also one of high sensitivity to political, judicial, financial, and geopolitical factors that could define its performance in the coming months.

Conclusion

YPF is the key player in Argentine energy, not only for its performance but also for its privileged access to Vaca Muerta, one of the most important unconventional deposits in the world. As I analyzed, the company improved its results in recent years, showing increases in EBITDA and cash generation and profitability, combined with a reduction in financial risk.

In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at significant discounts to the industry average, both in terms of earnings multiples, cash flow and enterprise value. From my point of view, this mismatch between price and fundamentals is very important considering the growth potential associated with productive expansion and infrastructure projects that will allow crude oil and gas exports to increase in the coming years.

Although the macroeconomic context is still challenging, I consider that the repositioning of YPF in financial and operational terms, added to a regulatory environment that is beginning to normalize, shows a high probability of progressive valuation. For long-term investors, I recommend buying, as I believe the balance between risk and return is favorable.

data.title: Nvidia: A Generational Investment With Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
My Nvidia History

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is my second-best investment of all time, where at its peak, it returned a 200% return in less than 2 years. My original investment was sparked by a combination of the company's repetitive early mover investments in GPUs, data centers, and the launch of CUDA, the most ubiquitous AI coding platform, giving Nvidia a huge moat. In late 2023, I looked at Nvidia's financials, their history of earnings, and valuation at the time. I was shocked it was trading at only ~26x earnings. It was also apparent AI was going to be "the next big thing" and Nvidia as the player. The next day, I put about 30-40% of my portfolio into buying shares & shorting long-dated puts. It paid off rather quickly. The investment was based on relative valuation, the same method I use to judge investments in both the most boring companies as well as the flashiest.

Current Environment

Nvidia is among the few generational companies that are directly tied to an industrial disruption. Nvidia doesn't just sell chips, it sells the entire computing stack. It owns the architecture, controls the software, and supplies the hardware that every major cloud provider and enterprise relies on. This level of vertical integration is rare and already led to pricing power and high margins. The business model has shifted over the years from a cyclical semiconductor company, tied to gaming demand. This shift explains why the company's revenue mix changed drastically over the last five years and why its valuation has expanded. Gross margin reached a record 75% in Q4 FY25 (ending January 2025). This marks a significant jump from the 65% range last year and the company's historical average of 60%. I expect the 75% level to be maintained in the foreseeable future as the companies new Blackwell line ships out in volume. Looking at the bottom line, profit margin climbed significantly from ~20% to 55%.

Recently, at the GTC 2025, Jensen Huang reinforced Nvidia's competitive positioning as the backbone of the AI economy. Huang touched on several interesting topics, including the concept of "AI Factories", advancements in physical AI/robotics, and the Launch of Dynamo AI OS. The industry is growing fast and Nvidia is keeping up speed. Most tangibly, he reiterated that the launch of the Blackwell architecture is pushing the envelope in terms of performance and efficiency. Production began in Q1 '25, and they're designed for large-scale AI workloads. Early revenue has started, but the full ramp up is expected in the second half of 2025 when volume shipments go out. The market's response is overwhelmingly positive so far with major customers lining up, including Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META) and other cloud service providers.

Nvidia is not a company whose valuation climbed as the result of a bubble. It has a long history of profitability, and is realizing results today which make the company worth owning. For as exciting of a business Nvidia is, I invest based on valuation which is supporting attractive entry points today.

Historical Performance

I always start my analysis process with a review of historical performance, typically the last 5 years. As we are into 2025 or FY26, with the fiscal year ending in January '26, I'm using FY21 as the base period. See below:

If 2025 plays out as planned on a per-share basis, sales per share will have increased over 12x, cash flow and earnings will both increase over 21x+, and book value 9x. This represents huge annualized earnings growth between 55-91% a year. What I find most noteworthy here, is that Nvidia's share price is trailing its true growth at only a 17x increase, a fraction of its cash flow and earnings. This is one of many flags a company is undervalued.

Future Performance

Given Nvidia's size today, it will not keep up the same annualized rates of growth, but they'll trend on the higher end relative to other companies. I expect they'll continue to grow all fundamentals between 10-12% on an annualized basis out to FY31. I think it's easy to get carried away on future projections, and I prefer to base my investments on lower-end assumptions.

My FY26 EPS estimates are quite conservative. I'm projecting EPS of $4.37 vs. $4.51, Wall Street's average. Also, considering Nvidia typically beats Wall Street expectations, they are likely to beat my earnings estimate. Looking out to 2030, there aren't as many calls for earnings yet, but Wall Street estimates FY27 earnings at $6.41, and I'm using just $7.13 for my FY31 projections. This gives me an extremely high level of confidence that the price targets I set using my low-end estimates will hit. I use these growth rates in my final valuation, and I never want to overpay for a stock because I assumed it would magically grow indefinitely. If the investment case is still bright after all my adjustments, it's an easy decision for me to move forward.

Valuation

Nvidia is a high-growth company that will likely trade above its 10-year historical valuation in the coming years. I usually look at the average 5-10 year PE multiple and assume a reversion to those levels, but I'll incorporate a different method to determine an appropriate multiple for Nvidia. For reference, Nvidia traded at about 29x earnings the last 10 years and 36x the last 5. Clearly, multiple expansion is in play.

Determining An Appropriate Valuation Multiple

Considering reversions to the mean, and projected growth rates around 10% annualized, I see Nvidia's P/E falling somewhere between the 10-year average of 31x and the blended average of 44x. I think 35-37x earnings in 2025 and 2026 is a safe multiple, considering Nvidia topped out between 42-89x in each of the last 4 years. A reversion to 35x on FY25 EPS of $5.49 implies a price target of $153 or +36% from today. See below for my price targets.

To further support my strong buy thesis with more than multiple reversion, here is a simple Discounted Cash Flow analysis using conservative inputs:

  • EPS starts at $4.23 in FY25

  • Grows to $7.13 by FY30, implying ~10.3% annualized growth

  • Terminal multiple at 33x (well below the 5-year average of 58x)

  • Discount rate: 10% (based on a 4.3% risk-free rate + 5.5% equity risk premium)

This yields a fair value of $146/share, or ~30% upside from today's price of $112. Even without assuming upside surprises from Blackwell or broader AI adoption, this DCF demonstrates NVIDIA remains undervalued on fundamentals alone. Let's see how my price target stacks up against other sources.

Guru Focus is quantitative based and shows a fair value of $177 today for Nvidia, perfectly in between my calls for 2025 & 2026. Their chart nicely shows Nvidia routinely trades at or above their "GF Value" with plenty of disconnects. Today, marks one of the larger disconnects from true value.

Tip Ranks polls Wall Street analysts and provides their average price target at $175, in line with what I'd expect one year from now, as well as the GF Value. Notably, the range of estimates is quite wide between the $120 bearish target and $220 for the most bullish. As we'll see in my Best & Worst Buy chart, I think that range is completely reasonable to expect given Nvidia's historical valuation peaks and valleys. While I make my investments primarily anticipating the equity reaching my price target, it's helpful to have an idea of the floor and ceiling. Buy closer to the floor, sell closer to the ceiling, and you'll end up ahead more times than not.

Identifying The Best and Worst Entry Points for Nvidia

My P/E table is an objective historical method to estimate future trading ranges. The Best Buy row outlines the price and multiple paid by those buying at the absolute bottom of the given year based on its earning. The Worst Buy row shows the multiple paid by those who bought at the top. 2022 is certainly an outlier year, and given the large range in values, I'm using median levels to determine a reasonable range for 2025. This implies Nvidia may trade as low as 23x or as high as 70x earnings this year and based on the EPS estimate, $4.37, that gives a $99-303 trading range.

For as fundamentally sound as Nvidia is, it is subject to extreme volatility, especially amid the current trade war. I expect long-term appreciation; however, this is a company that will likely face huge price swings in the short term and may move drastically day after day. I think the trading range I provided gives a good idea of where the general floor and ceiling for the stock is, but looking at the specific highs and low P/Es for each year quickly shows this range can be exceeded in extreme scenarios.

Today, Nvidia trades at 22.1x FY25 earnings, well within "Best Buy" territory, and cheaper than anyone could have purchased in all of 2021. I establish positions at and below the best-buy median, allowing me some comfort knowing I'm not buying at the top. If Nvidia gets as high as 2023's peak, representing the lowest high in the last 4 years, it will fetch $184 or +91% above today's quote within the calendar year. A run up to 2024's high yields a $223 price or +132% from today. See for yourself below.

Transforming a 16% Share Gain Into a 98% Return

Nvidia offers sizable premiums and a plethora of expiration dates to choose from. I'll keep it simple and choose June '26, the closest date that allows long-term capital gains on this attractive opportunity. As Nvidia is a little higher than the floor I set and well below the ceiling, this is the perfect setup for a covered straddle. The covered straddle involves buying 100 shares and simultaneously selling a covered call and put at the same strike and expiration. This effectively lowers the initial capital outlay while providing a significant margin of safety.

I love the covered straddle because it allows me to immediately lower my cost basis and secure a high risk-adjusted return. Nvidia is currently trading at $107.57 at the time of writing. I'm setting up a $125 strike covered straddle position, +16% today's quote. 100 shares would run me $10,757 outright, but lowers to $5,767 after collecting my premiums. See below for the full trade mechanics:

With this type of strategy, only two scenarios are in play. Nvidia trades at or above the $125 strike, or below it in June 2026. The best-case scenario shows an easy 98% annualized gain from no price movement a year from now.

Best-Case Scenario

  • If stock closes at $125.00 or higher, an increase of 16%
  • The calls will be exercised
  • You'll sell your original 100 shares for $12,500
  • The Puts will expire worthless
  • Final Position = No Shares & $12,500
  • Cash on Cash Net Profit ($12,500 - $5,767) = $4,926
  • Total Return = 98%
  • Annualized Return over 434 Days = 98%
